channel.title: Even - A super concise theme for Hugo
channel.description: Recent content on Even - A super concise theme for Hugo

item.title: About
item.description: Hi I’m Sean. I’m a data scientist currently working in public health at Essex County Council. Before that I worked as a statistician at the Department of Health & Social Care, and before that I was a data scientist working in marketing at We Buy Any Car. This site is mostly just a place for me to put notes or writeups of any projects I’ve been working on. I mostly use R, and I try to make all the stuff on here reproducible - so you should (hopefully!

item.title: Lets make a map in R
item.description: There’s a lot of really great posts on mapping in R. This one from the Trafford Data Lab is the one I used when I was starting out in R, and is the post I recommend to anyone interested in making maps with R. Most posts use ggplot2 to make maps. Here, we’re going to use the tmap package. tmap makes thematic maps - which is a fancy way of saying ‘plotting data on a map’.

item.title: Random forests in pictures
item.description: Random forests are great. They’re easy to train, they don’t care about outliers, and they have very few assumptions. You can use random forests for almost any problem you come across and, chances are, you’ll end up with a fairly decent predictive model. But what do random forests actually do? You might have heard something about random forests being “an average of decision trees”, but what does that mean?

item.title: Museums, antisocial behaviour, and DAGs
item.description: If you’ve ever used linear regression, you’ve probably came across statements like “a one unit increase in \(x\) causes a \(\beta\) increase in \(y\)”. But is this always the right thing to say? How can you be sure that changing \(x\) causes \(y\) to change? A few months ago I went on a two day “causal inference workshop”, ran by the fabulous Economics Professor Scott Cunningham. I have zero background in economics, but Scott is a great teacher and I learnt a lot from the workshop (even if it did feel like drinking from a firehose at times!
